EDCS-05000379.translation: Belonging to the Minucii. From Appius Iunius Zethus.
EDCS-05000379.summary: This inscription is a very fragmentary funerary epitaph or ownership mark, possibly dating to the 1st century CE, mentioning the family name Minucii and an individual named Appius Iunius Zethus.

EDCS-08600303.translation: To my most dutiful son. He lived 10 years, 5 months. Epaphroditus, his father, justly made this.
EDCS-08600303.summary: This is a Roman funerary epitaph for a boy who died at age 10 years and 5 months, dedicated by his father Epaphroditus, likely from the Imperial period.

EDCS-08600309.translation: Vindicate yourself against him who harms you. Vindicate yourself, and if Gaius Statius harmed you, vindicate yourself against him. Pursue him, so that he wastes away until he dies, and anyone else. And if Pollio is complicit, pursue him too, so that he does not complete a year.
EDCS-08600309.summary: This is a magical curse tablet, likely from the 1st or 2nd century CE, intended to invoke supernatural retribution against specific individuals who had caused harm.

EDCS-09400447.translation: To Prim[--], prefect of the First Wing of Batavians, from the colonists of the colony Veneria Iulia Restituta Tertianorum of Aleria.
EDCS-09400447.summary: This is a dedication to a prefect of a cavalry unit (ala) from the veterans of the colony of Aleria, likely dating to the 1st or 2nd century CE.

EDCS-09701553.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. Titus Plaetorius Celer made this for Diodora Quinta, his dearest wife. She lived 40 years. She is buried here.
EDCS-09701553.summary: This Roman funerary epitaph records the death of Diodora Quinta, who lived 40 years, and was dedicated by her husband Titus Plaetorius Celer, likely dating to the 1st-2nd century CE.

EDCS-09701554.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. For Lucius Manlius Felix. He lived 40 years. Flavia Tyche, his dearest wife, made this.
EDCS-09701554.summary: This is a Roman funerary epitaph for Lucius Manlius Felix, who lived 40 years, dedicated by his wife Flavia Tyche, likely dating to the 1st-2nd century CE.

EDCS-09800221.translation: In honor of the holy Appianus. With the help of God, Paulus made this.
EDCS-09800221.summary: This is a dedication to a saint, Appianus, likely made in the later Roman Empire or early Christian period, by an individual named Paulus.

EDCS-12300220.translation: Emperor Caesar Vespasianus Augustus, chief priest, with tribunician power for the second time, Imperator for the sixth time, father of the fatherland, consul for the third time, designated consul for the fourth time, granted citizenship and the right of marriage to veterans who served in the Misenum fleet under Sextus Lucilius Bassus, who completed twenty-six or more years of service and were discharged at Paestum, whose names are inscribed below, to them, their children, and their descendants; and to those who were unmarried at the time citizenship was granted to them, or who subsequently married, only one each. On the Nones of April, during the consulship of Caesar, son of Augustus, Domitianus, and Gnaeus Pedius Cascus. Baslel, son of Turbel, from Gallinaria in the Sarnian region. Described and recognized from the bronze tablet fixed in Rome on the Capitol, on the platform of the altar of the Julian family. Tablet 3, page 6, location 19. // Emperor Caesar Vespasianus Augustus, chief priest, with tribunician power for the second time, Imperator for the sixth time, father of the fatherland, consul for the third time, designated consul for the fourth time, granted citizenship and the right of marriage to veterans who served in the Misenum fleet under Sextus Lucilius Bassus, who completed twenty-six or more years of service and were discharged at Paestum, whose names are inscribed below, to them, their children, and their descendants; and to those who were unmarried at the time citizenship was granted to them, or who subsequently married, only one each. On the Nones of April, during the consulship of Caesar, son of Augustus, Domitianus, and Gnaeus Pedius Cascus. Baslel, son of Turbel, from Gallinaria in the Sarnian region. Described and recognized from the bronze tablet fixed in Rome on the Capitol, on the platform of the altar of the Julian family. Tablet 3, page 6, location 19. // Tiberius Iulius Fabianus Cestianus. Gaius Iulius Cornelii Niger. Marcus Valerius Alexandrus. Alexander the Great of Macedon(?). Lucius Valerius Verus. Lucius Licinius Pudentus. Lucius Rufinus Chaerea.
EDCS-12300220.summary: This is a rescript of Emperor Vespasian, likely issued in 70-71 CE, granting citizenship and marriage rights to veterans of the Misenum fleet, followed by a list of names, possibly witnesses or recipients, including a reference to Alexander the Great.

EDCS-12800249.summary: This is a very fragmented inscription, possibly a funerary dedication or ownership mark, starting with the name Papius Romanus, likely Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-13301710.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. Gaius Caninius Germanus, veteran of the emperor, formerly a centurion of the Praetorian fleet at Ravenna, lived 90 years. Gaius Caninius Carpus made this for his patron (or friend) deserving of well, and for himself, his freedmen, freedwomen, and their descendants.
EDCS-13301710.summary: This is a funerary epitaph dedicated by Gaius Caninius Carpus for his patron (or friend) Gaius Caninius Germanus, a high-ranking veteran, and for himself and his household, likely dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-13800116.translation: To the departed spirits, Solovius Leuc[unknown] official [unknown] priesthood [unknown].
EDCS-13800116.summary: This fragmentary funerary inscription records the name and some titles of the deceased, Solovius Leuc[unknown], and dates to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-14700006.translation: To the departed spirits, sacred. Iunia Tertulla, pious, lies here; she lived 24 years. Apronius Felix, soldier of the Praetorian fleet of the Pious Vindex at Misenum, chief herald(?), for his incomparable wife.
EDCS-14700006.summary: This funerary epitaph records the death of Iunia Tertulla, erected by her husband Apronius Felix, a soldier and herald of the Misenum fleet, likely dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-22500812.translation: To Gaius Caesar, son of Augustus, Emperor Caesar, son of the deified [Augustus], Pontifex Maximus, Consul for the 11th time, Imperator for the 12th time, with tribunician power for the 12th time. The councilors and citizens of the colony Veneria Pacensis Restituta Aleria, to their patron. [To Lucius Caesar, son of Augustus].
EDCS-22500812.summary: This inscription honors Gaius Caesar (Caligula) and Lucius Caesar, as indicated by the titles and the dedication by the decurions and colonists of Aleria, likely dating to the early Imperial period.

EDCS-22500813.translation: Sacred to the departed spirits. For Tettia Materna, an excellent wife, Lucius Iulius Longinus, procurator of the Emperor, erected this.
EDCS-22500813.summary: This funerary epitaph records the death of Tettia Materna, erected by her husband Lucius Iulius Longinus, a procurator, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-22500815.translation: Emperor Caesar Vespasian Augustus sends greetings to the magistrates and senators of the Vanacini. Regarding my friend and procurator Otacilius Sagitta, he has presided over you in such a way that he deserved your testimony. I am pleased; concerning the dispute over boundaries which you have with the Marians, pending from those lands which you bought from my procurator Publilius Memorialis, that it be settled by Claudius Clemens, my procurator. I have written to him and sent a surveyor. I confirm the benefits granted to you by the deified Augustus after his seventh consulship, which you maintained into the time of Galba. The envoys have acted: Lasemo, son of Leucanus, priest of Augustus; Eunus, son of Tomasius, priest of Augustus. On the 4th Ides of October, with Gaius Arruntius Catellius Celer and Marcus Arruntius Aquila as consuls for the fourth time.
EDCS-22500815.summary: This official decree from Emperor Vespasian addresses a boundary dispute between the Vanacini and Marians, confirming earlier grants and appointing a procurator to resolve the issue, dated to 69 AD.

EDCS-22500817.translation: To the departed spirits. For Gaius Baebius Marinus, Baebius Martialis and Baebia Proba, their loving children, erected this for their most devoted father.
EDCS-22500817.summary: This funerary epitaph records the death of Gaius Baebius Marinus, erected by his children Baebius Martialis and Baebia Proba, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-23400488.translation: Lucius Gellius Niger, soldier from the Misenum fleet, served 18 years, lived 40 years, lies here.
EDCS-23400488.summary: This funerary epitaph records the military service and death of Lucius Gellius Niger, a soldier of the Misenum fleet, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-34700135.translation: To the departed spirits. Flavia Bitalis made this for her well-deserving husband, Mario Fuscianus, with 27 years' stipend.
EDCS-34700135.summary: This funerary epitaph records the death of Mario Fuscianus, erected by his wife Flavia Bitalis, likely indicating his military service duration, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-34700136.translation: To the departed spirits. For Domitia Zonica, who lived about 30 years. Flavius Isidorus erected this for his deserving wife.
EDCS-34700136.summary: This funerary epitaph records the death of Domitia Zonica, erected by her husband Flavius Isidorus, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-34700137.translation: Publius Naevius Apollonius, freedman of Publius. Publius Naevius Castor, son of Publius. Naevia Dioclea, freedwoman of Publius. And of the freedmen and Titus Castor.
EDCS-34700137.summary: This inscription lists members of a family, likely freedmen and their descendants, with indications of patronage, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-34700138.translation: To Tiberius Claudius Caesar Augustus Germanicus, Pontifex Maximus, holding tribunician power, Imperator, Consul-designate, Eunus, son of Tatius, the priest, dedicated this at his own expense.
EDCS-34700138.summary: This inscription records the dedication of a monument by Eunus, a priest, to Emperor Claudius in the 1st century CE.

EDCS-36400233.translation: Emperor Caesar Trajan Hadrian Augustus, son of the deified Trajan Parthicus, grandson of the deified Nerva, Pontifex Maximus, holding tribunician power for the 12th time, Consul for the 3rd time, Father of the Fatherland. To those who served in the Praetorian fleet at Misenum, which is under Julius Frontonus, having completed twenty-six years of service and granted an honorable discharge, whose names are inscribed, he granted citizenship and the right of marriage with the wives they then had when citizenship was granted to them, or if they were unmarried, with those whom they would later marry, but only one wife each. On the twelfth day before the Kalends of March, when Publius Juventius Celsus II and Quintus Julius Balbus were consuls. From a common sailor, Marcus Cominius Vielonus Corus, son of Cubestus of Cobasia(?), and his daughter Taia, and his daughter Tatia. This was copied and recognized from a bronze tablet which is fixed in Rome in the precinct behind the temple of deified Augustus, near the temple of Minerva.
EDCS-36400233.summary: This is an official grant of citizenship and marriage rights by Emperor Hadrian to retired sailors of the Praetorian fleet at Misenum, dated to 118 CE.

EDCS-51400898.summary: This is a tomb inscription recording the maker of a funerary monument, likely a slave, in the Roman period.

EDCS-69200264.translation: To Aulus Petr[---], Augustal, Scutaria [---], Scutari [---], her husband.
EDCS-69200264.summary: This is a dedication from a wife to her husband, possibly a freedman of the imperial cult (Augustalis), during the Roman imperial period.

EDCS-69200277.translation: [---]RC[---], son of Gaius Irsius [---], made it.
EDCS-69200277.summary: This fragmentary inscription likely records the maker of a monument and their patronymic, from the Roman period.

EDCS-69200282.translation: To the spirits of the underworld for Euphrosyne.
EDCS-69200282.summary: This is a brief funerary inscription dedicated to Euphrosyne, from the Roman period.

EDCS-69200283.translation: To the spirits of the underworld. For Frontonus, dearest son. His parents erected this. He lived 17 years.
EDCS-69200283.summary: This is a moving funerary epitaph for a young boy named Frontonus, set up by his grieving parents, dating to the Roman imperial period.

EDCS-69200292.translation: [---], Iulia, daughter of Tiberius [---], [cared for it(?)]
EDCS-69200292.summary: This fragmentary inscription names a woman, Iulia, daughter of Tiberius, and suggests she was responsible for the monument, from the Roman period.

EDCS-69200301.summary: This is a very fragmentary inscription that seems to name at least one individual and possibly a familial relationship, from the Roman period.

EDCS-69200305.summary: This fragmentary inscription names Lucius Lartidius and indicates a paternal relationship, dating to the Roman period.

EDCS-69200307.summary: This is a funerary inscription that begins with the name Lucius, possibly with additional letters, dated to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-69200310.translation: Cottus, of Lucius Piso, in Corsica. To Xanti.
EDCS-69200310.summary: This inscription likely records a slave or freedman named Cottus belonging to Lucius Piso, perhaps indicating his location in Corsica and dedicating something to Xanti, dated to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-69200311.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. To Vulcatius Hospes, father.
EDCS-69200311.summary: This is a funerary inscription dedicating a monument to Vulcatius Hospes, who is identified as the father of the deceased, dated to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-69200314.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. To A[e]gnatia Marcri[n]a, formerly by name, having lived as a resident, she died after 3 years.
EDCS-69200314.summary: This is a funerary inscription dedicated to A[e]gnatia Marcri[n]a, noting her former status as a resident and her lifespan, dated to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-71200629.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. To -dio M- - Claudius(?).
EDCS-71200629.summary: This is a fragmented funerary inscription that begins with the invocation to the departed spirits and includes partial names, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-71200671.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. To Marcus [3]tro, from his mother.
EDCS-71200671.summary: This is a funerary inscription from a mother dedicating a monument to her son, Marcus [3]tro, dated to the Roman Imperial period.
